threats.title: MinterUpgradeable calculate_rebase Underflow Due to smNFT Double-Counting
threats.hypothesis_description: The calculate_rebase function double-counts smNFT balances, leading to an arithmetic underflow that permanently blocks epoch emissions.
threats.investigation_rationale: If smNFTs are minted, their balance is included in both _veTotal and _smNFTBalance. This causes veBlackSupply to exceed blackSupply, resulting in an underflow when calculating circulatingBlack. This blocks update_period() and halts the protocol.
preconditions: smNFTs are minted and locked in the ve contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Double addition of smNFT balance in supply calculation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None, triggered by normal user action (minting smNFTs)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any epoch flip after sufficient smNFTs are minted

operational_questions: Does _veTotal include smNFT balances?
What is the threshold of smNFTs required to trigger the underflow?
validation_requirements: Test calculate_rebase with simulated smNFT balances
Verify if update_period reverts under these conditions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users legitimately mint smNFTs over time
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The combined balance of regular veNFTs and smNFTs grows
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: At epoch flip, update_period calls calculate_rebase
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to underflow, permanently halting emissions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None, happens naturally as protocol grows

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of protocol emissions and epoch transitions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix the math
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All token minting and gauge distributions

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Sufficient to cross the underflow threshold

threats.title: BlackGovernor Empty Clock Implementations Break Governance Snapshotting
threats.hypothesis_description: The clock() and CLOCK_MODE() functions have empty implementations, returning default values (0 and empty string), which breaks the OpenZeppelin Governor snapshot mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: OZ Governor relies on clock() to determine the current timepoint for proposal snapshots. Returning 0 means all proposals use timepoint 0, where no one has voting power, rendering governance completely non-functional.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function clock() public view override returns (uint48) {}

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Empty function bodies for critical OpenZeppelin overrides

operational_questions: How does the OZ Governor handle a clock() returning 0?
Can any proposal ever reach quorum or pass?
validation_requirements: Deploy BlackGovernor and attempt to create and vote on a proposal
Check the snapshot timepoint recorded for the proposal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt to vote, but their voting power at timepoint 0 is zero

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Governance is completely broken and unusable
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires deploying a new Governor contract

threats.title: Bribes Withdraw Silent Failure Desynchronizes Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function in Bribes.sol silently fails if the requested amount exceeds the tracked balance, allowing users to retain phantom voting power in the bribe contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the Voter contract attempts to withdraw more votes than the Bribe contract has recorded (due to rounding or logic differences), the withdrawal is skipped. The Voter clears its state, but the Bribe contract retains the balance, allowing the user to earn rewards indefinitely without active votes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) { ... } // No else or revert

preconditions: User has deposited votes in the Bribe contract
Voter calls withdraw with an amount slightly larger than balanceOf[tokenId]

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Silent failure on withdrawal condition instead of reverting

operational_questions: Can a user manipulate their veNFT balance to cause the Voter to withdraw a larger amount?
Does the Voter contract ignore the silent failure?
validation_requirements: Test vote reset with manipulated veNFT balances
Verify if Bribe balance remains after Voter reset

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker votes on a gauge, depositing balance X into the Bribe contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker manipulates their veNFT (e.g., adding to lock) to increase voting power to Y (Y > X)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker resets their vote. Voter calls Bribe.withdraw(Y)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdrawal silently fails. Attacker retains balance X in Bribe but has 0 votes in Voter
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires specific sequence of voting and locking

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of bribe rewards from legitimate voters
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High, requires upgrading the Bribe contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users with 0 active votes but non-zero Bribe balances
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Bribe totalSupply mismatching Voter pool weights
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Frequent vote resets after lock additions

threats.title: Bribes Earned Unbounded Loop Causes Permanent DoS for Reward Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: The earned() function iterates over all epochs since the user's last claim. With 30-minute epochs, this loop grows rapidly and will exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol uses WEEK = 1800 seconds (30 minutes), meaning 48 epochs pass per day. If a user waits 30 days to claim, the loop runs 1440 times, performing multiple state reads per iteration, causing an Out-Of-Gas revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) { ... _index = getPriorBalanceIndex(...) }

preconditions: User has unclaimed rewards spanning a large number of epochs

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded iteration over time periods for reward calculation

operational_questions: What is the exact gas cost per iteration of the earned loop?
How many days of inactivity result in a permanent lock on Avalanche?
validation_requirements: Simulate a claim after 30 days of inactivity
Measure gas consumption of getReward

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User deposits votes and earns bribes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User forgets or chooses not to claim for several weeks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User attempts to claim rewards via GaugeManager.claimBribes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to exceeding block gas limit

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of user funds (bribe rewards)
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High, requires contract upgrade to implement partial claims

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed claimBribes transactions with OOG errors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large amounts of unclaimed tokens in Bribe contracts

threats.title: Permissionless Gauge Creation Enables Global Fee Distribution DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can spam the permissionless createGauge function to bloat the pools array, causing the distributeFees() function to exceed the block gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: distributeFees() iterates over the entire pools array. Since anyone can create a gauge for any whitelisted token pair, an attacker can create hundreds of dust pools and gauges, making the automated epoch upkeep revert due to OOG.
preconditions: Attacker has funds to create many cheap LP pairs and gauges

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded loop over an array that can be appended to permissionlessly

operational_questions: How many gauges are required to hit the Avalanche block gas limit?
Is there a whitelist restriction on pair creation that mitigates this?
validation_requirements: Test gas consumption of _distributeFees per pool
Verify token handler whitelist enforcement on gauge creation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys many whitelisted token pairs (if possible) or uses existing ones
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls createGauge for each pair, bloating the pools array
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts, blocking fee distribution for all legitimate pools

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Global halt of LP fee distribution to internal bribes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium, keepers can use the paginated distributeFees(start, finish) but automated upkeep will fail

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic scripting to deploy pairs and gauges

threats.title: Missing Gauge Validation in claimRewards Allows Arbitrary Code Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: The claimRewards function does not verify if the provided gauge address is legitimate, allowing an attacker to pass a malicious contract that executes arbitrary logic in the context of GaugeManager.
threats.investigation_rationale: By passing a malicious contract as _gauge, the attacker forces GaugeManager to call getReward() on it. This can be used to spoof msg.sender == gaugeManager to other protocol contracts (like BribeFactoryV3) or reenter GaugeManager.
preconditions: Attacker deploys a malicious contract implementing getReward

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External call to user-provided address without validation

operational_questions: Which protocol contracts trust GaugeManager as msg.sender?
Can the malicious contract call BribeFactoryV3.createBribe to deploy rogue bribes?
validation_requirements: Deploy a malicious gauge and attempt to call restricted functions on BribeFactoryV3 via claimRewards
Test reentrancy into GaugeManager state-changing functions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys MaliciousGauge with a custom getReward function
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: MaliciousGauge calls BribeFactoryV3.createBribe(), bypassing the onlyAllowed modifier since msg.sender is GaugeManager

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Creation of rogue protocol components or state corruption
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Access control relying on GaugeManager identity

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to claimRewards with unknown gauge addresses

threats.title: Governance Quorum Manipulation via Current Supply Reads
threats.hypothesis_description: The quorum calculation uses the current total supply of smNFTs rather than the historical supply at the proposal's snapshot time, allowing attackers to manipulate the quorum threshold during active voting.
threats.investigation_rationale: By minting or burning smNFTs after a proposal is created, an attacker can artificially inflate or deflate the quorum requirement, causing legitimate proposals to fail or malicious proposals to pass with fewer votes than intended.
preconditions: Attacker has the ability to mint or burn smNFTs during the voting period

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Using current state (no timestamp argument) for historical governance checks

operational_questions: Can smNFTs be minted permissionlessly by users?
What is the cost to mint enough smNFTs to significantly alter the quorum?
validation_requirements: Test quorum changes before and after minting smNFTs during an active proposal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: A legitimate proposal is submitted and voting begins
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker observes the proposal is close to passing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker mints a large number of smNFTs, increasing the total supply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The quorum threshold rises above the current vote count, causing the proposal to fail

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Legitimate governance actions (like Minter.nudge) can be blocked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium, requires deploying a fixed Governor

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large smNFT mints during active proposals

threats.title: Corrupted Event Data in External Bribe Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: The _setExternalBribe function emits the internal bribe address instead of the external bribe address as the old value, corrupting off-chain monitoring and indexing.
threats.investigation_rationale: Off-chain tools, subgraphs, and governance dashboards rely on events to track protocol state. Emitting the wrong address breaks historical tracking of external bribes and can hide malicious admin changes.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Copy-paste error in event emission parameters

operational_questions: Do any off-chain systems rely on this event for critical UI or accounting?
Can this be used to obscure a malicious bribe change?
validation_requirements: Check subgraph or indexer logic for SetBribeFor event handling

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or malicious GAUGE_ADMIN changes external bribe to a rogue contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Event emits the internal bribe address as the old value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Off-chain monitors looking for changes to specific external bribes miss the alert

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of off-chain state integrity and monitoring blind spots

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: SetBribeFor events with mismatched old values

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Epoch Emissions When Total Voting Weight is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: If notifyRewardAmount is called when total voting weight is zero, the transferred base tokens are permanently locked in the GaugeManager because the global reward index is not updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: During early protocol phases or if all users reset their votes, totalWeight can be 0. The Minter transfers emissions to GaugeManager, but since _ratio is 0, the index doesn't increase. There is no sweep function to recover these tokens.
preconditions: Epoch flips and Minter calls notifyRewardAmount

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Tokens transferred before state update conditions are validated
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Epoch flip when votes are zero

operational_questions: Is it practically feasible for totalWeight to be 0 in production?
Can an attacker force totalWeight to 0 via flash-resets?
validation_requirements: Test epoch flip with 0 votes
Verify tokens remain stuck in GaugeManager

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Protocol launches or experiences a period where all users reset votes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Keeper triggers epoch flip via performUpkeep
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Minter mints tokens and sends them to GaugeManager
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: GaugeManager index does not update. Tokens are permanently locked

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of an entire epoch's emissions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High, requires contract upgrade to sweep tokens

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: GaugeManager base token balance increasing without index increase
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Zero emissions distributed for an epoch
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Mass vote resets before epoch flip

threats.title: Missing GenesisManager Initialization Blocks Epoch Upkeep
threats.hypothesis_description: The EpochController initialize function does not set the genesisManager address. If not manually set before the first epoch flip, the automated upkeep will revert, blocking all emissions.
threats.investigation_rationale: performUpkeep calls genesisManager.checkAtEpochFlip(). If genesisManager is address(0), the call reverts. Deployment scripts do not show setGenesisManager being called on EpochController, creating a high risk of deployment failure.
deployment_assumptions: Deployer forgets to call setGenesisManager after initialization
threats.config_evidence: V1/upgradeable/deployKeeper.js deploys EpochController but does not call setGenesisManager.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical dependency omitted from initialize function

operational_questions: Is there an off-chain runbook that ensures setGenesisManager is called?
What happens to emissions if the epoch flip is delayed?
validation_requirements: Review full deployment sequence
Test performUpkeep with uninitialized genesisManager

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Chainlink Automation attempts to call performUpkeep
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts. Emissions are halted until admin intervenes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Halt of epoch transitions and emissions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low, admin just needs to call setGenesisManager

threats.title: Admin Recovery Function Causes Bribe Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: The recoverERC20AndUpdateData function in BribeFactoryV3 calls emergencyRecoverERC20 on the Bribe contract instead of the correct update function, leaving the Bribe's internal accounting inflated.
threats.investigation_rationale: When an admin attempts to recover tokens and update the data, the factory calls the wrong function. The tokens are removed, but tokenRewardsPerEpoch is not reduced. Users will try to claim rewards based on the inflated accounting, causing transactions to revert due to insufficient balance.
preconditions: Admin calls recoverERC20AndUpdateData to fix a token issue

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Function name implies data update, but underlying call does not update data

operational_questions: Under what circumstances would the admin use this function?
How can the insolvency be resolved once triggered?
validation_requirements: Test admin recovery flow
Verify user claims revert after recovery

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin legitimately attempts to recover accidentally sent tokens using recoverERC20AndUpdateData
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Tokens are transferred to the owner, but Bribe accounting remains unchanged
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt to claim their earned bribes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bribe contract lacks sufficient balance, causing claims to revert for all subsequent users

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent DoS on bribe claims for the affected token
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High, requires admin to manually refund the Bribe contract

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed getReward transactions due to insufficient balance

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Causes Fee Distribution DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The setReferralFee function lacks an upper bound check. If set above 1000 (100%), the fee calculation in GaugeCL will underflow, permanently blocking LP fee distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: dibsPercentage is used to calculate the referral fee: claimed0 * referralFee / 1000. If referralFee > 1000, the calculated fee exceeds the claimed amount. The subsequent subtraction (claimed0 -= _dibsFeeToken0) will revert due to arithmetic underflow.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing bounds check on critical fee parameter

operational_questions: Is there a UI validation that prevents setting this > 1000?
What happens to accumulated fees while distribution is blocked?
validation_requirements: Set fee to 1001
Attempt to call distributeFees and verify underflow revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or mistaken GAUGE_ADMIN calls setReferralFee(1500)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: GaugeCL._claimFees calculates dibs fee > claimed amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts on underflow, blocking all CL gauge fee distributions

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Halt of LP fee distribution to internal bribes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low, admin can reset the fee to a valid value
